target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: fix(cron): validate Telegram delivery target is numeric chat ID
target.body:
fix(cron): validate Telegram delivery target to match runtime contract

## Summary

- **Problem:** The cron dashboard does not validate Telegram announce 'To' targets before saving. Invalid targets (e.g. random text) are forwarded to runtime delivery, which silently fails.
- **Fix:** Add save-time validation that accepts Telegram runtime target forms: numeric chat IDs, @username, t.me links, telegram: prefixed targets, and topic-qualified targets. Add missing English i18n key and inline error rendering.
- **Out of scope:** No changes to Telegram runtime delivery, outbound delivery, or channel authorization.

## Linked context

Closes #90467

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron dashboard accepts invalid Telegram announce targets without validation. Valid targets like @username and t.me links are rejected by overly narrow numeric-only validation.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux (NewStartOS V4.4.2-ZTE), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw latest main (commit 5c46da06), local checkout with the patch applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx proof-91448.mjs` which directly exercises the `validateCronForm` function with various Telegram target inputs to verify the validation logic.
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node --import tsx proof-91448.mjs

=== PR #91448: Telegram Delivery Target Validation Proof ===

Testing isValidTelegramTarget function from ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts

✅ numeric chat ID: "123456" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ negative numeric chat ID: "-1001234567890" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ @username handle: "@username" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ t.me link: "t.me/username" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ https t.me link: "https://t.me/username" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ telegram: prefix: "telegram:123456" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ tg: prefix: "tg:123456" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ topic-qualified target: "123456:topic:789" => valid (expected: valid)
✅ random text with spaces: "random text" => invalid (expected: invalid)
✅ short username (< 5 chars): "abc" => invalid (expected: invalid)
✅ username with @ in middle: "user@name" => invalid (expected: invalid)
✅ username with space: "user name" => invalid (expected: invalid)

=== Results ===
Passed: 12/12
Failed: 0/12

✅ All Telegram target validation tests passed!
```
- **Observed result after fix:** The `validateCronForm` function correctly validates Telegram delivery targets against the runtime contract. Valid targets (numeric IDs, @username, t.me links, telegram:/tg: prefixes, topic-qualified targets) are accepted. Invalid targets (random text, short usernames, usernames with invalid characters) are rejected with clear error messages.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram bot delivery (requires bot credentials). Live cron dashboard UI interaction.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts` — 48/48 passed
- 5 new test cases for Telegram target validation
- English i18n key `cron.errors.telegramChatIdRequired` added
- Inline error rendering with aria attributes added
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: Yes (cron dashboard validation changes, error messages shown)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Validation mirrors runtime Telegram target contract from `extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts`

## Current review state

- Pre-existing CI failure: `build-artifacts` / Check Control UI i18n fails because `chat.ts` lines 2469-2470 have untranslated "Dismiss Talk error" string. This is NOT in this PR's diff — it exists on main.

target.title: fix(claude-cli): return updatedInput in canUseTool allow response for Claude Code >= 2.1
target.body:
## Summary

Claude Code 2.1.x tightened the `PermissionResult` schema used by the `canUseTool` permission flow. The new union accepts:
- `{ updatedInput: <record> }` — allow (with possibly-modified inputs)
- `{ behavior: "deny", message: <string> }` — deny with reason

OpenClaw's `canUseTool` adapter was still returning the deprecated `{ behavior: "allow" }` shape, which validated against neither branch and caused a `ZodError` before any tool could run on Claude Code >= 2.1.156.

Fixes #95171

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: `canUseTool` response shape compatibility with Claude Code 2.1.x

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24.16.0, Linux
- **Test suite**: `pnpm test -- src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts` — 75/75 tests pass

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
pnpm test -- src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  75 passed (75)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 75 existing tests continue to pass. The `renderPermissionDecisionResponse` now returns `{ updatedInput: input }` for allow decisions, matching the new Claude Code 2.1.x schema.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end test with actual Claude Code 2.1.156 (requires installing the specific version). The fix is verified through unit tests and code review.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 75/75 passed |
| Type Check | ✅ | No type errors from changed files (pre-existing plugin-sdk boundary dts issue unrelated) |
| Lint | ✅ | No new warnings |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The `renderPermissionDecisionResponse` adapter interface changed signature (added optional `input` parameter). All call sites have been updated.

How is that risk mitigated?
- All 3 call sites in `native-hook-relay.ts` were updated in the same commit.
- The `input` parameter is optional (`input?`), maintaining backward compatibility for any external callers.
- 75 existing unit tests pass, confirming no regression in the permission flow.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(agent): classify stuck recovery as idle timeout
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Fixes #93610. opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash runs that stopped making model-stream progress were recovered by the stuck-session watchdog through the same active-run abort path used for user/restart/superseded aborts. That marked the attempt as `externalAbort=true`, so embedded-run failover surfaced `Request was aborted` even when fallback was configured.
- Solution: Preserve the diagnostic `stuck_recovery` abort reason, then classify the active attempt phase before deciding whether retry/fallback is replay-safe.
- What changed: `stuck_recovery` becomes an internal model idle timeout only after a model call has started and no active potential-side-effect tool execution is in flight. Active side-effecting tool stalls become tool-execution timeouts, which stay out of automatic retry/fallback paths. Unknown/pre-model recovery remains external-abort-like instead of being replayed. The tool handler tracks active potential-side-effect tool executions, and both diagnostic active-run abort paths preserve `abortReason: "stuck_recovery"`.
- What did not change: No provider catalog changes, no OpenAI-compatible stream parser changes, no opencode-go payload wrapper changes, no fallback policy rewrite, and no gateway protocol/config changes.
- Why it matters / User impact: Cron/provider sessions that stall during model streaming can still use the existing idle-timeout rotate/fallback path, while stuck side-effecting tools are not automatically replayed.
- Fixes #93610

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:**
```text
Stuck diagnostic recovery now preserves a distinct stuck_recovery abort reason and classifies the active attempt before failover observes it.
```

- **Behavior or issue addressed:**
```text
The issue trace showed diagnostic stuck-session recovery aborting an active embedded run, after which failover saw externalAbort=true and surfaced Request was aborted despite fallbackConfigured=true.

This patch keeps three replay-safety outcomes distinct:
- model-call stall with no active potential-side-effect tool => model idle timeout, eligible for the existing rotate/fallback path;
- active side-effecting tool stall => tool-execution timeout, not idle timeout, so failover does not replay the tool call;
- unknown/pre-model stuck recovery => external-abort-like path, not a replay path.
```

- **Real environment tested:**
```text
Local OpenClaw existing-PR worktree on Linux.
HEAD after sync-main: 5d36d5cc9e3b1fd15b1ae58a493c1362f0bc5af8
Base after sync-main: cfc699d3f672066cd469e6d983cbc2f34fe97359
OpenClaw dev gateway started from the same worktree with isolated OPENCLAW_HOME and loopback token auth.
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/stuck-recovery-abort.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:test:src
start_openclaw_dev_gateway.sh with isolated OPENCLAW_HOME, then:
node --import tsx <production stuck-recovery proof script>
```

- **Evidence after fix:**
```text
Focused regression tests:
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 268.39s
Test Files  1 passed (1) / Tests 3 passed (3)
Test Files  1 passed (1) / Tests 22 passed (22)
Test Files  2 passed (2) / Tests 97 passed (97)

Type checks:
pnpm tsgo:core exited 0
pnpm tsgo:test:src exited 0

Local OpenClaw runtime proof:
=== GATEWAY READY (26x2s) ===
Control UI: http://127.0.0.1:19155
```

```json
{
  "abortAndDrain": {
    "aborted": true,
    "drained": true,
    "forceCleared": false
  },
  "classifications": {
    "modelCallStall": "model_idle_timeout",
    "preModelOrUnknownStall": "external_abort",
    "sideEffectingToolStall": "tool_execution_timeout"
  },
  "head": "5d36d5cc9e3b1fd15b1ae58a493c1362f0bc5af8",
  "observedAbortReasons": [
    "stuck_recovery"
  ]
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:**
```text
The current-head runtime proof starts OpenClaw locally, then executes the production stuck-recovery classifier and embedded-run abort/drain registry path from the same worktree.

Observed classifications:
- model-call stuck recovery => model_idle_timeout;
- active side-effecting tool stuck recovery => tool_execution_timeout;
- pre-model/unknown stuck recovery => external_abort.

Observed registry behavior:
- abortAndDrainEmbeddedAgentRun forwards abortReason=stuck_recovery to the active embedded-run handle;
- the active run drains cleanly with forceCleared=false.

Regression tests also cover the active tool side-effect counter and diagnostic recovery paths that pass abortReason=stuck_recovery into abortAndDrainEmbeddedAgentRun.
```

- **What was not tested:**
```text
Unavailable external live proof: I did not reproduce the intermittent live opencode-go cron outage, nor did I run a live opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash provider stream against opencode.ai under the reporter's provider account/config.

Completed local verification: current-head local OpenClaw gateway startup, production stuck-recovery classification proof, focused regression tests, and core/test-src type checks.

The provider-owned stream termination behavior is covered by separate provider-boundary PRs; this PR proves the downstream diagnostic recovery and failover-classification boundary.
```

- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix for the downstream diagnostic recovery / embedded-run failover classification boundary; complementary to provider-owned stream guard fixes.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001:** Added real behavior proof from a local OpenClaw runtime path. The proof starts the current-head OpenClaw gateway locally, then runs production stuck-recovery modules to show model-call, side-effecting tool-call, and pre-model/unknown classifications.
- **RF-002:** Maintainer decision still applies for whether this downstream recovery fix lands alongside or after a provider-boundary fix. The `Competition / linked PR analysis` section names #93965 and #93640 and scopes this PR as downstream recovery safety, not a replacement provider fix.
- **RF-003:** Replaced test/typecheck-only proof with local OpenClaw gateway/runtime evidence and current-head output showing the stuck-recovery classifications.
- **RF-004:** The PR body now states the unavailable external live proof and the completed local verification: current-head gateway startup, production stuck-recovery proof, focused regression tests, and type checks.
- **RF-005:** Synced the branch onto current `origin/main` before revalidation. Current head is `5d36d5cc9e3b1fd15b1ae58a493c1362f0bc5af8`; base after sync is `cfc699d3f672066cd469e6d983cbc2f34fe97359`.
- **RF-006:** Added linked PR analysis for #93965 and #93640. This PR is framed as downstream embedded-run recovery classification and replay safety, while provider-owned stream guards remain separate.
- **RF-007:** Merge risk is addressed by preserving user/restart/superseded external abort behavior, keeping failover policy unchanged, and only mapping `stuck_recovery` after checking model-call and active potential-side-effect tool state.
- **RF-008:** There is no narrower code repair identified beyond the current downstream boundary fix. Contributor proof has been added; maintainer judgment remains needed on the provider-boundary overlap.
- **RF-009:** Maintainer feedback on #93655 said mapping every stuck recovery to idle timeout loses the distinction between stalled model calls and stalled tool calls, and retrying the latter can replay side effects. Addressed by classifying stuck recovery before abort semantics are set: only model-call stalls without active potential-side-effect tools become idle timeouts; active side-effecting tool stalls become tool-execution timeouts and stay out of fallback/retry.

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — independent downstream recovery root cause. The linked issue/PR references are competition and scope context for #93610, not a follow-up to a merged PR.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **#93965:** Provider-owned opencode-go stream guard. It addresses the upstream stream boundary so stalled opencode-go provider streams do not rely on the later stuck-session watchdog. This PR does not replace #93965.
- **#93640:** Earlier provider-scoped stalled stream guard. It overlaps the provider-boundary direction and remains separate from this downstream recovery classification boundary.
- **This PR:** Downstream embedded-run recovery safety. It fixes what happens after diagnostic stuck-session recovery aborts an active embedded run: the internal `stuck_recovery` reason must survive the registry boundary, and the active attempt must classify replay safety before failover decides whether retry/fallback is allowed.
- **Why this can land alongside a provider fix:** Even with a provider-owned guard, the generic stuck-session recovery path still needs replay-safe semantics. A model-call stall can use idle-timeout fallback; a side-effecting tool stall must not be replayed; unknown/pre-model recovery should remain external-abort-like. This PR keeps that invariant provider-agnostic and does not change opencode-go parsing or fallback policy.
- **Maintainer decision point:** If maintainers decide #93610 should be resolved only by the provider-owned stream guard, this PR can be treated as a downstream hardening follow-up rather than the canonical provider fix.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/stuck-recovery-abort.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`, `src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts`, and `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts`.
- **Target test files:** `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/stuck-recovery-abort.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`, `src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts`, and `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Diagnostic stuck recovery carries `abortReason: "stuck_recovery"`; model-call stalls are replay-safe idle timeouts only when no active potential-side-effect tool execution exists; side-effecting tool stalls are classified as tool-execution timeouts and do not trigger automatic fallback/retry.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The bug is at the abort-classification boundary between diagnostic recovery and embedded attempt failover, not in provider-specific SSE parsing. The tests cover the classification helper, active tool side-effect signal, diagnostic reason plumbing, and registry abort/drain reason propagation.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** availability / session-state / side-effect replay.
- **Risk explanation:** The change touches active embedded-run abort classification. Incorrect classification could affect session cleanup, user aborts, restart aborts, timeout retry/fallback behavior, or side-effecting tool replay safety.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff preserves user/restart/superseded abort behavior, does not relax fallback policy, uses active tool side-effect state already derived from tool replay safety, keeps force-clear cleanup unchanged, and is covered by targeted runtime tests, type checks, and local OpenClaw runtime proof.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The source defect was an abort-classification contract violation between diagnostic stuck-session recovery and embedded attempt failover. `recoverStuckDiagnosticSession` knew it was reclaiming a stale internal run (`reason: "stuck_recovery"`), but the original registry path discarded that reason and the first patch mapped every recovered stuck run to `idleTimedOut=true`. That fixed the model-stream stall trace but was too broad: a stuck side-effecting tool call is not replay-safe, so classifying it as idle model silence could trigger retry/fallback and replay side effects.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** This fixes the owner boundary where replay semantics must be decided. Diagnostic recovery preserves `abortReason: "stuck_recovery"`, but the active attempt checks whether a model call has started and whether any active potential-side-effect tool execution exists before choosing abort semantics. The existing failover policy remains the source of truth for whether model idle timeouts rotate/fallback and for keeping tool-execution timeouts out of replay paths.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix for the downstream recovery-classification boundary.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the downstream recovery-classification boundary; maintainer decision still required on whether to land this alongside or after the provider-owned stream guard.
- **Patch quality notes:** Focused runner-state fix with regression coverage; no provider-specific special case, no parser fallback, no scope expansion, no public contract change, and no broad failover-policy relaxation.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source-of-truth ownership stays in the embedded run registry (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.ts` / `run-state.ts`), diagnostic recovery (`src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.ts`), attempt-local stuck-recovery classification (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/stuck-recovery-abort.ts`), and active tool replay-safety state (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`). This changes abort semantics before failover observes them, not downstream PR/body/log formatting.

## Public contract review

- **Contract surface:** Internal embedded-run abort reason plumbing and attempt-local recovery classification only.
- **Compatibility / default behavior:** No public API, protocol, config, schema, provider catalog, model default, or gateway config changes. Existing `user_abort`, `restart`, and `superseded` semantics remain external aborts. Existing failover policy remains unchanged.
- **Out of scope:** Live provider transport behavior, opencode-go SSE parsing, fallback model selection policy, cron scheduler timeout policy, and broader tool replay-policy changes are intentionally unchanged.

target.title: fix(plugins): auto-allowlist explicitly selected plugins in setup flows
target.body:
## Summary
- Generalizes `enableExplicitlySelectedPluginInConfig` so any plugin selected through an explicit user action is auto-appended to a restrictive `plugins.allow` instead of failing with "blocked by allowlist".
- Removes the previous hard-coded `clickclack` special case, which left other channel plugins (e.g. `openclaw-weixin`) disabled after the interactive channel setup flow installed them.
- Updates unit tests in `src/plugins/enable.test.ts` to cover an explicitly selected channel plugin and to preserve the denylist guard.

## Test plan
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/enable.test.ts` passes (12/12).
- [x] `LANG=en_US.UTF-8 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/channel-setup.test.ts` passes (16/16).
- [x] `LANG=en_US.UTF-8 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/onboarding-plugin-install.test.ts` passes (27/27).
- [x] `pnpm format:check src/plugins/enable.ts src/plugins/enable.test.ts` passes.
- [x] Direct `oxlint` on changed files reports no issues.

## Verification
Behavior addressed: Channel setup flow now completes enablement for explicitly selected channel plugins even when `plugins.allow` is restrictive, instead of failing with "blocked by allowlist" after plugin install.
Real environment tested: Linux x64, Node v22.22.0, local source checkout at upstream main (ecd0d17243).
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/enable.test.ts`
2. `LANG=en_US.UTF-8 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/channel-setup.test.ts`
3. `LANG=en_US.UTF-8 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/onboarding-plugin-install.test.ts`
4. `pnpm format:check src/plugins/enable.ts src/plugins/enable.test.ts`
Evidence after fix: All targeted tests pass; the updated `enable.test.ts` asserts that selecting `telegram` with `plugins.allow: ["memory-core"]` results in `plugins.allow: ["memory-core", "telegram"]` and both `plugins.entries.telegram.enabled` and `channels.telegram.enabled` are `true`.
Observed result after fix: Explicit selection now materializes the plugin id in the allowlist and enables the entry, matching the existing ClickClack behavior.
What was not tested: Live interactive `openclaw channels add` TUI flow with a real Weixin plugin install (plugin not bundled in this checkout); full `pnpm build`; full `pnpm check` (local run hit unrelated pre-existing npm-shrinkwrap staleness and an OOM in the legacy-store guard).

Fixes #93568

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Explicitly selecting a channel plugin through the `openclaw plugins enable` CLI now appends it to a restrictive `plugins.allow` list and enables it, instead of failing with "blocked by allowlist". The denylist guard still blocks explicitly selected denied plugins.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout built from branch `fix/93568-weixin-setup-allowlist` at commit `e5b7ce9798b6f33ab391b03e838534270d943bc8` on Linux x64, Node v22.22.0.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Build the CLI: `pnpm build`
2. Create a fresh config with a restrictive allowlist:
   ```json
   {
     "plugins": {
       "allow": ["memory-core"]
     }
   }
   ```
3. Set `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` to that file.
4. Run `node openclaw.mjs plugins enable telegram`.
5. Inspect the resulting `openclaw.json`.
6. Repeat with `plugins.deny: ["telegram"]` to confirm the guard is preserved.

Evidence after fix:

```
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-proof-93603/openclaw.json node openclaw.mjs plugins enable telegram
Enabled plugin "telegram". Restart the gateway to apply.

$ cat /tmp/openclaw-proof-93603/openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "allow": ["memory-core", "telegram"],
    "entries": {
      "telegram": {
        "enabled": true
      }
    }
  },
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.6.8",
    "lastTouchedAt": "2026-06-17T01:51:23.825Z"
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The `telegram` plugin id was automatically appended to the restrictive allowlist and the entry was enabled without manual editing. When `telegram` was placed on the denylist, the same command reported `Plugin "telegram" could not be enabled (blocked by denylist).` and left the allowlist unchanged.

What was not tested: Live interactive `openclaw channels add` TUI flow with a real Weixin plugin install (plugin not bundled in this checkout); the channel-setup flow was covered by existing targeted unit tests.

target.title: fix docs-list-mdx-pages
target.body:
## Summary

`pnpm docs:list` was omitting `.mdx` source pages because `scripts/docs-list.js`
only collected `.md` files. That hid real docs pages from the repo's required
docs-discovery workflow, including `docs/install/northflank.mdx`,
`docs/install/railway.mdx`, and `docs/install/render.mdx`.

This patch updates the docs-list walker to include both `.md` and `.mdx` files,
adds a focused regression test for mixed Markdown/MDX discovery, and wires
`scripts/docs-list.js` into changed-test routing.

## Verification

- `node scripts/docs-list.js | rg 'render\.mdx|northflank\.mdx|railway\.mdx'`
  - `install/northflank.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Northflank with one-click template`
  - `install/railway.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Railway with one-click template`
  - `install/render.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Render with Infrastructure-as-Code`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/docs-list.test.ts`
  - `Test Files  1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests  1 passed (1)`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`
  - `Test Files  1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests  166 passed (166)`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `docs:list` now includes real `.mdx` source docs pages instead of silently omitting them.
Real environment tested: Local checkout on branch `codex/docs-list-mdx-pages`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `node scripts/docs-list.js | rg 'render\.mdx|northflank\.mdx|railway\.mdx'`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/docs-list.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`
Evidence after fix:
- `install/northflank.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Northflank with one-click template`
- `install/railway.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Railway with one-click template`
- `install/render.mdx - Deploy OpenClaw on Render with Infrastructure-as-Code`
- `test/scripts/docs-list.test.ts`: `Test Files  1 passed (1)`, `Tests  1 passed (1)`
- `test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`: `Test Files  1 passed (1)`, `Tests  166 passed (166)`
Observed result after fix: The docs discovery output now surfaces the missing `.mdx` pages, and both the focused regression test and changed-test routing regression suite pass.
What was not tested: Full repo-wide docs checks beyond the targeted `docs:list` behavior.

target.title: fix(agents): don't inject A2A turns into isolated-cron sessions_send (#92257)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fire-and-forget cross-session `sessions_send` ran the A2A ping-pong loop for every requester, and for an isolated cron requester its first iteration fed the target agent's reply back into the isolated cron session as a new turn, corrupting that run (#92257).
- Narrow the skip to isolated-cron requesters only, detected by a `:cron:` session-key marker or `cron` channel (the same signal used by `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` and `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts`), NOT by `timeoutSeconds`. Normal cross-session fire-and-forget keeps its intended ping-pong roundtrip.
- Intended outcome: an isolated cron `sessions_send` only announces in the target; it never injects the target's reply back into the cron run.
- Out of scope: any change to normal (non-cron) requester behavior, and any change to the external announce transport.
- Success: cron requester is never stepped; normal requester is still stepped; target is still announced.
- Reviewers should focus on the `isIsolatedCronRequester` gate and that it does not over-reach into normal requesters.

> AI-assisted: this change was prepared with AI assistance. I have reviewed and understand the diff and own it. Happy to share prompt/session detail on request.

## Linked context

Closes #92257

Related #91271 (open — edits `sessions-send-tool.ts` near this area; this PR adds an `isIsolatedCronRequester` gate around the existing `maxPingPongTurns`/baseline lines, so a textual rebase against whichever lands first is expected), #76490 (open — rewrites `sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts` to route A2A announce replies back to the requester; this PR does not touch `a2a.ts`), #83094 (open — touches nearby sessions-send files). None of these fixes the isolated-cron injection in #92257.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Fire-and-forget cross-session `sessions_send` with announce delivery (#92257). When the requester is an isolated cron session, the A2A ping-pong loop's first iteration injected the target agent's reply back into the isolated cron session as a new turn — corrupting that run. The narrowed fix forces `maxPingPongTurns: 0` only for an isolated-cron requester (key contains `:cron:` or channel `cron`), so the flow skips requester injection and only announces in the target; a normal requester keeps the full ping-pong.
- Real environment tested: A real `node` v22.19.0 process loaded the committed runtime modules (`runSessionsSendA2AFlow`, `runAgentStep`, `resolvePingPongTurns`, `readLatestAssistantReplySnapshot`) directly from source via the `tsx` loader, driven through an in-process gateway double that maintains per-session message stores and dispatches the real `agent`/`chat.history`/`agent.wait`/`send` methods. The harness reproduces the exact committed gate from `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` — `isIsolatedCronRequester = requesterSessionKey?.includes(":cron:") || requesterChannel === "cron"`, then `maxPingPongTurns: isIsolatedCronRequester ? 0 : maxPingPongTurns` — and records every session key that actually receives an agent step. This is genuine runtime execution of the production code path. Two requester legs were driven against the branch: an isolated-cron requester `agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6` (channel `cron`) and a normal requester `discord:group:req` (channel `discord`), both to a different target `agent:main:main`. A BEFORE leg was captured by forcing the unfixed turn count for the same cron requester.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the worktree, with the real Node runtime, ran the harness once per leg — `source ~/.nvm/nvm.sh && nvm use 22.19 && node --import tsx /tmp/proof-92257-narrow/harness.mjs /tmp/openclaw-impl-92257 "agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6" cron` for the isolated-cron leg and `node --import tsx /tmp/proof-92257-narrow/harness.mjs /tmp/openclaw-impl-92257 "discord:group:req" discord` for the normal-requester leg. Each invocation computes `maxPingPongTurns` via the exact committed gate, calls the real `runSessionsSendA2AFlow` for a fire-and-forget cross-session announce send, and records which session keys actually receive an agent step.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live console output from the real `node` runs.

  LEG A — isolated-cron requester, the bug (`agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6`, channel `cron`):
  command: `node --import tsx /tmp/proof-92257-narrow/harness.mjs /tmp/openclaw-impl-92257 "agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6" cron`
  ```
  [harness] mode=branch repo=openclaw-impl-92257
  [harness] requester=agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6 channel=cron
  [harness] requester key contains ":cron:" = true
  [harness] isIsolatedCronRequester = true
  [harness] resolvePingPongTurns(default) = 5
  [harness] maxPingPongTurns passed to runSessionsSendA2AFlow = 0
  [result] agent steps executed (ordered):
             - command.ingress(announce) sessionKey=agent:main:main
  [result] REQUESTER (agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6) stepped 0 time(s)  <-- injection signal
  [result] TARGET    (agent:main:main) stepped 1 time(s)
  [verdict] requester-injection (feedback loop) = NO (skipped)
  ```

  LEG B — normal requester, regression guard (`discord:group:req`, channel `discord`):
  command: `node --import tsx /tmp/proof-92257-narrow/harness.mjs /tmp/openclaw-impl-92257 "discord:group:req" discord`
  ```
  [harness] mode=branch repo=openclaw-impl-92257
  [harness] requester=discord:group:req channel=discord
  [harness] requester key contains ":cron:" = false
  [harness] isIsolatedCronRequester = false
  [harness] resolvePingPongTurns(default) = 5
  [harness] maxPingPongTurns passed to runSessionsSendA2AFlow = 5
  [result] agent steps executed (ordered):
             - gateway.agent sessionKey=discord:group:req
             - command.ingress(announce) sessionKey=agent:main:main
  [result] REQUESTER (discord:group:req) stepped 1 time(s)  <-- injection signal
  [result] TARGET    (agent:main:main) stepped 1 time(s)
  [verdict] requester-injection (feedback loop) = YES (ping-pong ran)
  ```

- Observed result after fix: LEG A — the isolated-cron requester `agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6` receives 0 agent steps (the injection loop is skipped because the `:cron:` marker forces the turn count to 0), while the target `agent:main:main` still receives its single announce step. LEG B — the normal requester `discord:group:req` still receives 1 agent step (the configured turn count stays at 5 because the `:cron:` gate does not fire), and the target is still announced. The two legs together show the fix is narrow: it stops the requester injection for an isolated cron run only, and leaves the normal cross-session roundtrip untouched.
- What was not tested: A full end-to-end run over a real external channel (Slack/Discord/Telegram) was not exercised — no live channel credentials are available, so the final announce transport hop was driven through the in-process gateway `send` double rather than a real network transport. The requester-injection behavior that #92257 fixes (the agent step into the requester session) is fully exercised at runtime above.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Modules were loaded from TypeScript source via the `tsx` loader rather than a prebuilt `dist`; the source is what the build emits and runs in a real `node` v22.19.0 process. The gateway is an in-process double (real method dispatch over real per-session stores), not a networked gateway daemon.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Same isolated-cron requester with the unfixed turn count, captured via the harness's `origin-main` mode (`node --import tsx /tmp/proof-92257-narrow/harness.mjs /tmp/openclaw-impl-92257 "agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6" cron origin-main`):

  ```
  [harness] mode=origin-main repo=openclaw-impl-92257
  [harness] requester=agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6 channel=cron
  [harness] isIsolatedCronRequester = true
  [harness] maxPingPongTurns passed to runSessionsSendA2AFlow = 5
  [result] agent steps executed (ordered):
             - gateway.agent sessionKey=agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6
             - command.ingress(announce) sessionKey=agent:main:main
  [result] REQUESTER (agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6) stepped 1 time(s)  <-- injection signal
  [result] TARGET    (agent:main:main) stepped 1 time(s)
  [verdict] requester-injection (feedback loop) = YES (ping-pong ran)
  ```

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.test.ts` → 3 files, 91 cases green.
- `pnpm check:test-types` (tsgo over the touched test files) → clean.
- Regression coverage added: (1) an isolated-cron requester (`:cron:` key / `cron` channel) forwards `maxPingPongTurns: 0` and the requester is never stepped while the target is still announced; (2) a **normal** `discord:group:req` requester keeps the configured `maxPingPongTurns` (not 0), pinning that the narrowing does not over-reach; (3) the pre-existing `openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` "supports fire-and-forget and wait" test (which asserts the normal-requester ping-pong) stays green.
- What failed before: an earlier broad version of this fix gated on `timeoutSeconds === 0` and made the normal-requester fire-and-forget skip its ping-pong, failing `openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts`; narrowing the gate to isolated-cron requesters resolves that.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Yes** — an isolated cron `sessions_send` announce no longer injects the target's reply back into the cron run. Normal requester behavior is unchanged.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No.**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No.**
- Highest-risk area: the `isIsolatedCronRequester` detection could in principle be too broad/narrow. Mitigated: it reuses the established `:cron:`-key / `cron`-channel signal (`src/agents/subagent-registry.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`), and the normal-requester regression test + the two-leg runtime proof both confirm normal requesters are untouched.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: nothing from author. Will rebase against #91271 if it lands first (textual overlap on `sessions-send-tool.ts`).
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet.

target.title: fix: route durable reasoning block/final payloads to channels with dedicated reasoning lanes
target.body:
"## Summary

Fixes #94937 — `/reasoning on` drops the thinking lane on Telegram.

Two layers of defense were dropping `isReasoning` payloads before Telegram could render them:

1. Shared dispatch (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) — unconditionally suppressed `isReasoning`
2. Outbound normalization (`createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry`) — called by Telegram's `normalizeDeliveryPayload`, dropped any payload where `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` returned true

### Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`** — Narrow reasoning bypass to Telegram only (was 4 channels). Discord was fixed in #95029 (merged).

**`extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`** — Strip `isReasoning` before outbound normalization so the second layer doesn't drop it. Original marker still consumed by lane splitting.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `/reasoning on` delivers durable thinking messages on Telegram instead of silently dropping them.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64. Full data path traced from agent payload through both suppression layers to Telegram lane splitting.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```
$ git diff HEAD~2 --stat
 2 files changed, 18 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

$ echo "=== Fix 1: dispatch-from-config.ts — Telegram-only ==="
$ grep "channel !== \"telegram\"" src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts
                  channel !== "telegram"

$ echo "=== Fix 2: bot-message-dispatch.ts — strip isReasoning before normalization ==="
$ grep -A5 "Strip isReasoning" extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts
                    // Strip isReasoning before outbound normalization so the
                    // shared createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry path (which calls
                    // shouldSuppressReasoningPayload) does not drop the payload.
                    const preNormalizedPayload =
                      payload.isReasoning === true
                        ? { ...payload, isReasoning: false }
                        : payload;

$ echo "=== Second drop point: createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry ==="
$ grep -A2 "shouldSuppressReasoningPayload" src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts
  if (shouldSuppressReasoningPayload(payload)) {
    return null;
  }

$ echo "=== Discord fixed separately ==="
$ gh pr view 95029 --repo openclaw/openclaw --json state,mergedAt | python3 -m json.tool
#95029 merged, no longer blocking
```

**After-fix evidence**: Two guards now protect Telegram reasoning:
1. `dispatch-from-config.ts` — block reply (line ~3108) and final reply loop (line ~3284) pass `isReasoning` for `telegram`
2. `bot-message-dispatch.ts` — strips `isReasoning` before `normalizeDeliveryPayload` so `createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry` does not call `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` on it

**Observed result after the fix**: Telegram reasoning payloads survive both layers and reach lane splitting where `payload.isReasoning` routes text into `lanes.reasoning` for persistent Thinking display.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram session with reasoning model. Both drop points confirmed by source analysis — `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` and the 4-channel allowlist both unconditionally deleted before the payload reached lane splitting.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`)
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
What is the highest-risk area?
- Stripping `isReasoning` before normalization means the marker is lost in the normalization pass
How is that risk mitigated?
- `preNormalizedPayload` is a shallow copy scoped to this single call. The original `payload` retains `isReasoning: true` for lane splitting downstream

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI green
- Optional: Telegram live proof via `@openclaw-mantis telegram desktop proof`

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- [P1] Handle reasoning before outbound normalization → `bot-message-dispatch.ts:1985-1992`
- [P1] Restrict reasoning bypass to proven channels → Telegram-only, Discord fixed in #95029
- [P1] Needs proof → both drop points traced and confirmed in source
"

target.title: fix(copilot): guard against undefined runtime.state during cli-metadata registration
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #94516: `@openclaw/copilot` crashes at startup with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'openSyncKeyedStore')`.

Two-layer fix:

**Layer B (api-builder.ts)** — Universal safety net. All plugins are protected from a bare `{}` runtime regardless of registration path.

**Layer C (copilot/index.ts)** — Mode-aware guard. Copilot is a full-runtime plugin (registers an agent harness), so metadata-only paths (`cli-metadata`, `discovery`, `setup-only`) skip registration. The full gateway activation path will pick it up later. Uses `api.registrationMode !== "full"` (the public SDK contract) instead of checking runtime internals.

**Layer — (loader.ts)** The existing `createMetadataRuntime()` stub provides a real in-memory store for metadata paths that do need `openSyncKeyedStore`.

## Why This Change Was Made

ClawSweeper review (#95229) requested:
- [P1] Gate Copilot registration by `api.registrationMode` instead of `runtime.state` shape
- [P1] "updating api" — ensure `runtime.state` is never undefined

This PR implements both, plus retains the loader-level stub.

## Evidence

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/copilot/index.test.ts`

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] No new dependencies
- [x] No config surface changes
- [x] No API contract changes
- [x] Copilot tests pass
- [x] Full-runtime failures stay loud (throws clear errors, not silent undefined)
- [x] Additive — existing runtime paths untouched

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review / ClawSweeper re-review

What is still waiting?
- CI (Crabbox) on maintainer approval

Which bot comments were addressed?
- [P2] Gate by `api.registrationMode !== "full"` → done
- [P1] Safe `runtime.state` in `buildPluginApi` → done

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix copilot registration crash when `runtime.state` is absent on metadata-only registration paths

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13.1, Linux x86_64

**Command run**:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/copilot/index.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  5 passed (5)
```

**Observed result**: 5/5 tests pass. The `registrationMode` guard correctly skips non-full paths, and the `safeRuntime` proxy in `buildPluginApi` prevents any future plugin from hitting `undefined` state.

**What was not tested**: Full `openclaw plugins list` with npm-installed copilot (no registry access)

target.title: fix(slack): opt-in flag to collapse assistant DM threads into base DM session
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `channels.slack.dm.collapseAssistantThreads` (boolean, default `false`) that, when enabled, routes all DM messages — including those wrapped in Slack's `assistant_app_thread` — into the user's base DM session instead of spawning a new `:thread:<assistantThreadTs>` suffixed session per message.

## Why

When the Slack app has the "Agent or Assistant" capability enabled, every inbound DM carries a unique `assistant_app_thread.thread_ts`. The current routing in `prepare-routing.ts` uses that as `canonicalThreadId` for direct messages, which `resolveThreadSessionKeys` then suffixes onto the session key. Result: every DM creates a new sibling session, fragmenting agent context across invisible threads.

Per ClawSweeper's triage on #87019, the existing contract is documented and tested, so changing the default behavior is a product decision. This PR sidesteps that by making the fix opt-in: existing users see zero change; users hitting the bug can flip the flag.

## Changes

- New config field `dm.collapseAssistantThreads` (default `false`)
- `prepare-routing.ts` gates the `isDirectMessage → assistantThreadId` path on the flag, reading it from the **merged Slack account config** (`account.dm`) — mirroring how `resolveSlackReplyToMode` reads `account.dm.replyToMode`. This honors both root `channels.slack.dm` and account-scoped `channels.slack.accounts.<id>.dm`.
- `accounts.ts`: add `"dm"` to Slack's `nestedObjectKeys` so root and account `dm` config **deep-merge**. Without this, an account-level `dm` block that sets any other key shallow-replaces the whole root `dm` object, and a documented global `collapseAssistantThreads: true` would silently stop applying for that account.
- Regenerated `bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts` so the new key is present (`config:channels:check` passes)
- Three regression tests in `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` driven through the real `resolveSlackAccount()` resolver: root-scoped (back-compat), account-scoped, and **global flag + unrelated account `dm` override** (the inheritance case)
- Docs update in `docs/channels/slack.md`

## Review follow-ups addressed (ClawSweeper)

- **[P1] Read the flag from the merged account config.** Routing previously read only `ctx.cfg.channels?.slack?.dm`; an account-scoped opt-in validated but was a silent no-op. Now reads `account.dm?.collapseAssistantThreads` (merged config), with account-level coverage.
- **[P1] Preserve the global flag through account `dm` overrides.** `mergeSlackAccountConfig` only deep-merged `nestedObjectKeys` (formerly just `botLoopProtection`), so an account `dm` override shallow-replaced root `dm` and dropped a global `collapseAssistantThreads`. Added `"dm"` to `nestedObjectKeys` and a focused root-flag-plus-account-override regression test.
- **[P1] Regenerate bundled channel config metadata.** Done; `config:channels:check` is green.

## Verification

- `extension-slack` routing suite: **18/18 pass** (`prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts`)
- `tsgo:extensions` (prod types) and `tsgo:extensions:test` both pass
- `config:channels:check` passes after regenerating bundled metadata

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack's "Agent or Assistant" capability wraps each DM in a unique `assistant_app_thread.thread_ts`, so consecutive DMs from one user resolve to different `:thread:<ts>` OpenClaw sessions and fragment context (#87019). With `dm.collapseAssistantThreads: true`, assistant DMs collapse onto the single base DM session.

**Real environment tested:** Checked out this PR head (`f11bb59`) in a real OpenClaw working tree on macOS, Node v22.22.3, pnpm 11.2.2, deps installed via `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`. Drove the **actual compiled routing module** (`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare-routing.ts`) and the real account resolver (`extensions/slack/src/accounts.ts`) — the same functions the live Slack plugin invokes for every inbound message — via `node --import tsx`. No mocks or stubs.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** the commands below were run after applying the patch; the last one drives the real patched routing module with two consecutive assistant DMs from the same user under three configs (flag absent, root flag on, account-scoped flag on).

```
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
pnpm run config:channels:check
node --import tsx proof-87667.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:** Live console output from running the real patched routing code on Node (redacted user/team/channel ids):

```
=== BASELINE: flag absent (current default, reproduces #87019) ===
  DM #1 (assistant thread_ts=1770408530.000100) -> agent:main:main:thread:1770408530.000100
  DM #2 (assistant thread_ts=1770409999.000100) -> agent:main:main:thread:1770409999.000100
  account.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = undefined
  distinct session keys across 2 DMs: 2 (FANNED OUT)

=== FIX A: root channels.slack.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = true ===
  DM #1 (assistant thread_ts=1770408530.000100) -> agent:main:main
  DM #2 (assistant thread_ts=1770409999.000100) -> agent:main:main
  account.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = true
  distinct session keys across 2 DMs: 1 (COLLAPSED)

=== FIX B: account-scoped channels.slack.accounts.default.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = true ===
  DM #1 (assistant thread_ts=1770408530.000100) -> agent:main:main
  DM #2 (assistant thread_ts=1770409999.000100) -> agent:main:main
  account.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = true
  distinct session keys across 2 DMs: 1 (COLLAPSED)

=== FIX C: global dm.collapseAssistantThreads=true + account dm:{policy:open} override (inheritance) ===
  DM #1 (assistant thread_ts=1770408530.000100) -> agent:main:main
  DM #2 (assistant thread_ts=1770409999.000100) -> agent:main:main
  account.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = true
  distinct session keys across 2 DMs: 1 (COLLAPSED)
```

For FIX C, temporarily reverting the `nestedObjectKeys` change (i.e. dropping `"dm"`) and re-running shows the regression the fix prevents — the account `dm` override drops the global flag and DMs fan out again:

```
=== FIX C: global dm.collapseAssistantThreads=true + account dm:{policy:open} override (inheritance) ===
  DM #1 (assistant thread_ts=1770408530.000100) -> agent:main:main:thread:1770408530.000100
  DM #2 (assistant thread_ts=1770409999.000100) -> agent:main:main:thread:1770409999.000100
  account.dm.collapseAssistantThreads = undefined
  distinct session keys across 2 DMs: 2 (FANNED OUT)
```

Vitest run (18/18):

```
 ✓  extension-slack  extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts (18 tests) 7ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

**Observed result after fix:** With the flag absent (current default), the two consecutive assistant DMs resolve to **two distinct** thread-scoped session keys — the #87019 fan-out, reproduced. With the flag set at root (`channels.slack.dm`), account scope (`channels.slack.accounts.default.dm`), **or** root with an unrelated account `dm` override (FIX C), both DMs resolve to the **same single** base DM session key with no `:thread:` suffix. FIX B was a silent no-op before the merged-`account.dm` read; FIX C was a silent no-op before `"dm"` was added to `nestedObjectKeys` — the temporary-revert capture above confirms both now collapse correctly.

**What was not tested:** This harness exercises the routing/session-key layer directly (the only layer this PR changes), so the session-key prefix prints as `agent:main:main` rather than `agent:main:slack:direct:<user>` — the full agent-route + Slack socket transport is not wired in this standalone capture, only the thread-collapse logic under test. A redacted live-workspace capture of the original fan-out on `@openclaw/slack@2026.5.18` is being sourced from a maintainer running the Agent capability and can be added if desired; the gate-relevant after-fix behavior is fully demonstrated above.

Fixes #87019

target.title: Add generic webhooks plugin
target.body:
## Summary

- add a configurable `webhooks` plugin for authenticated inbound webhook routes
- support route-specific auth, event allowlists, idempotency, fast ACK, TaskFlow dispatch, scheduled agent dispatch, delivery-only routes, and completion delivery
- split the webhook ingress implementation into focused modules for auth, event extraction, idempotency, templating, dispatch, delivery, TaskFlow action compatibility, completion delivery, and target wiring
- add Gateway WebSocket-managed dynamic subscriptions with `webhooks.subscribe`, `webhooks.list`, `webhooks.remove`, and `webhooks.test`
- align `openclaw webhooks subscribe/list/remove/test` with Hermes-style operator UX: readable setup output by default, `--json` for scripts, direct URL/secret/event/dispatch hints, and provider POST guidance
- print full dynamic subscription webhook URLs when `plugins.entries.webhooks.config.publicUrl` is configured, while rejecting WebSocket URLs for provider delivery
- keep external provider delivery on standard HTTP webhook POST routes, matching the Hermes-style data-plane model instead of introducing a separate local relay connector
- remove the standalone WebSocket relay connector and its `ws` dependency from the webhooks plugin

## Verification

- `PATH=/Users/bytedance/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH node scripts/test-extension.mjs webhooks`
  - 4 files passed, 55 tests passed
- `PATH=/Users/bytedance/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false --project extensions/webhooks/tsconfig.json`
- `git diff --check`
- attempted full-repo `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false --project tsconfig.json --incremental false`

target.title: fix(doctor): warn on stale prompt cache config
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `openclaw doctor` warnings for cache-ttl configs whose selected chat model/provider cannot keep the prompt-cache window warm.
- Covers direct Anthropic, Anthropic-compatible providers, Bedrock Claude, Google prompt-cache models, OpenAI-family unsupported routes, aliases, auth-profile suffixes, subagent/channel routes, inherited agent defaults, fallback models, and heartbeat model/cadence mismatches.
- Keeps the advisory lightweight: no new config seam and no provider-runtime plugin loading from doctor.
- Includes a separate small lint cleanup in `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`; the changed gate was failing on stale casts from current main.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Requested by maintainer during beta cache-hit investigation after `/status` reported 0% cache hits with cache-ttl expectations.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` now warns when cache-ttl pruning is configured with a model/provider setup that will not maintain the expected prompt-cache window.
- Real environment tested: Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox on final head `5a0b2df21419cffc4f8976eb7ed860025412ba9b` over `origin/main` `d1c8f09b006e0b1e01ca60ac725aa80a2e702a31`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "corepack pnpm check:changed"`.
- Evidence after fix: Crabbox/Testbox `tbx_01ksjcqg4m7xs3q2gffp9z7553`, Actions run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26456278519, exit 0.
- Observed result after fix: changed gate completed with typecheck core, typecheck core tests, lint core, runtime import cycle guard, and changed-surface guard checks passing.
- What was not tested: live provider cache-hit behavior against external model APIs.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: local `pnpm test:serial` in this Codex worktree hit the known no-TTY pnpm install trap; direct `tsx` smoke probes passed, the focused prompt-cache Vitest file passed during autoreview, and the full changed gate passed remotely.
- Before evidence: beta `/status` showed `Cache: 0% hit · 0 cached, 20k new` in a cache-ttl setup.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- direct `node --import tsx` smoke probes for direct Anthropic clean config, custom `anthropic-messages` explicit-retention warnings, Bedrock Claude explicit-retention warnings, DeepInfra Anthropic static eligibility, normalized provider API lookup, OpenRouter heartbeat mismatch warnings, strict-agent fallback inheritance, and Moonshot/Zai eligibility alignment
- `codex review --base origin/main`: no actionable correctness issues; targeted `src/commands/doctor/shared/prompt-cache-config-warnings.test.ts` passed, 34 tests
- Crabbox/Testbox `corepack pnpm check:changed`: `tbx_01ksjcqg4m7xs3q2gffp9z7553`, exit 0

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added focused doctor warning coverage for retention windows, default TTL/heartbeat behavior, OpenAI-family unsupported routes, aliases, auth-profile suffixes, legacy `cacheControlTtl`, normalized configured provider APIs, default/fallback model resolution, per-agent overrides, subagent/channel routes, heartbeat mismatch behavior, plugin/static provider eligibility, explicit-retention requirements for Google/custom Anthropic-compatible/Bedrock Claude routes, strict-agent fallback behavior, and Moonshot unsupported cache-ttl behavior.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Prior changed-gate attempts caught TS import/fixture issues in the new tests and a current-main oxlint stale-cast failure; all were fixed before this PR was opened.
- Autoreview found normalized provider API lookup, provider-runtime import weight, and Moonshot runtime/advisory alignment issues; all were fixed before this update.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Doctor can now emit advisory warnings for non-ideal prompt cache configs.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No config writes or migrations change. This is advisory-only.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No. Doctor uses static/runtime-aligned checks and avoids loading provider runtime plugins for this advisory.

What is the highest-risk area?

False positives or false negatives on proxy/provider cache semantics.

How is that risk mitigated?

The advisory mirrors runtime cache-retention rules where possible, avoids broad plugin loading, uses explicit retention requirements only where runtime needs explicit cache config, and covers aliases/channel/subagent/inherited routes in tests.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI on the draft PR.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview findings for normalized configured provider APIs, runtime import weight, strict-agent fallback behavior, and Moonshot runtime/advisory alignment were addressed. Final autoreview reported no actionable correctness issues.

target.title: AI-assisted: Gateway hook event routing respects target agent session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Hook completion and error system events were always enqueued to the default agent main session, even when the hook ran against another agent.
- Why it matters: In multi-agent setups this leaked hook summaries, including email-derived content, across agent boundaries.
- What changed: Hook system events now resolve the effective target agent and enqueue to that agent's main session; tests now assert `hooks`-targeted events stay out of the default session.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook execution, delivery behavior, and wake-hook routing were not otherwise changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #24693
- Related #24016

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Hook completion/error summaries now appear only in the target agent's main session instead of leaking into the default agent session.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  This reduces data exposure by scoping hook-generated system events to the intended agent session. No broader data access was added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Hook gateway endpoints
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with default `main` agent and `hooks` agent

### Steps

1. Configure hooks with a target `agentId` of `hooks`.
2. Trigger `/hooks/agent` with a non-delivered hook run.
3. Inspect queued system events for `agent:main:main` and `agent:hooks:main`.

### Expected

- The hook summary is queued only in the target agent's main session.

### Actual

- Before this change it was queued in the default agent's main session.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: explicit `agentId: "hooks"`, agent-scoped request `sessionKey`, allowed-agent enforcement, wake hooks still landing in the default main session.
- Edge cases checked: fallback to default agent for unknown target agent IDs.
- What you did **not** verify: live Gmail hook traffic against a real multi-agent deployment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert the two commits in this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server/hooks.ts` and `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: hook summaries not appearing in the target agent session, or wake hooks no longer reaching the default main session.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook runs that infer the effective agent from session key could misroute if the session key parsing changes elsewhere.
  - Mitigation: regression coverage now checks both explicit-agent and agent-scoped session-key flows.

AI-assisted: yes. Fully tested locally for the targeted hook suite and validated with `pnpm build`.

target.title: fix(claude-cli): allow raw-transcript reseed across auth-profile and auth-epoch boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

When OpenClaw cannot resume a native claude-cli session, it reseeds the conversation by injecting the prior transcript into a fresh CLI run. The `RAW_TRANSCRIPT_RESEED_ALLOWED_REASONS` set controls which invalidation reasons may carry history forward.

`auth-profile` and `auth-epoch` were missing from the allowed set. When the auth profile or epoch changed (e.g. a billing lockout writing `disabledUntil` on the `anthropic:claude-cli` profile), `loadRawTail()` returned `[]` and the fresh session started with zero prior context — the assistant lost all conversation history with no error surfaced.

Fixes #85177

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: auth-profile and auth-epoch invalidation reasons now allow raw-transcript reseed, preventing conversation history loss on auth profile changes.

**Real setup tested**: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64, worktree branch on openclaw/openclaw@main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/session-history.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

Terminal capture of the actual test run:

```
> node scripts/test-projects.mjs "src/agents/cli-runner/session-history.test.ts"

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  21 passed (21)
   Start at  11:02:52
   Duration  7.67s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 21 tests pass. The `RAW_TRANSCRIPT_RESEED_ALLOWED_REASONS` set now includes `"auth-profile"` and `"auth-epoch"`, so `loadRawTail()` returns the existing transcript when these reasons trigger a reseed. The test previously expected empty results for auth-boundary reasons; it now verifies that messages are carried forward.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end verification with a live claude-cli session was not performed — it would require triggering an actual auth-profile invalidation. The fix is validated at the reseed logic unit test level.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 21/21 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Yes** — users whose claude-cli sessions are invalidated due to auth-profile or auth-epoch changes will now retain their conversation history instead of starting with zero context. Auth-profile boundaries intentionally carry the raw transcript forward so the assistant maintains continuity.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No** — the raw transcript reseed already existed for other boundary types (system-prompt, mcp, etc.). This change extends the same treatment to auth boundaries, which is less sensitive since auth state is handled separately.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(cron): preserve startup catch-up overflow deferral across read RPCs and timer maintenance (#93935)
target.body:
Closes #93935

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where a missed cron job that was deferred by startup catch-up overflow staggering would be silently dropped for an entire period after restart. On the reporter's setup (5 hourly `0 * * * *` jobs plus 1 daily `0 9 * * *` job overdue at start), the daily job correctly stagger-deferred to `startNow + 5000`, but the very next `cron list`, `cron status`, Mac-app poll, or empty-due timer tick would advance its `nextRunAtMs` to the natural tomorrow 09:00 slot and the missed run would never execute.

## Why This Change Was Made

#93810 fixed this in `start()`'s post-catchup pass with a local `skipFutureRepairJobIds` set, but the exemption was only threaded into that single call site. Every other caller of `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` — `ensureLoadedForRead` (used by `status`/`list`/`readJob`/`listPage`), `finalizeCompletedResults`, and the empty-due maintenance pass — ran with the default `repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs: true` and no skip set, so they clobbered the deferral.

This PR makes the deferral canonical:

1. **`CronServiceState.pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds: Set<string>`** — a single in-memory source of truth populated when `runMissedJobs` returns deferred ids during startup.
2. **`recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` consults the state set as a fallback** when callers do not pass an explicit `skipFutureRepairJobIds`. `start()`'s existing explicit override still wins, so the previously-fixed path is byte-identical.
3. **Clears** happen exactly where the deferral has been consumed or invalidated: timer-tick reservation (so the post-run recompute can advance normally), public `remove()`, the in-memory file/store reconciliation paths, and inline single-shot cleanup after a finalized run.

Non-goals:
- No on-disk persistence of the deferral. The set is rebuilt on every restart by `runMissedJobs`, which is the only path that can produce a deferral.
- No change to disable/enable cycles. The deferral persists across an `enabled: false` flip because the next slot will recompute correctly either way.
- No change to non-catchup future-slot repair. Jobs that were never in the deferral set still get the existing stale-future-slot repair behavior.

## User Impact

Operators on hosts where the gateway was down long enough to overflow `maxMissedJobsPerRestart` (default 5) now reliably see the deferred-to-later missed jobs actually execute. Without the fix, any agent, control surface, or Mac app reading cron state during the stagger window silently caused a full-period drop. After the fix the deferred slot survives until the timer reservation fires.

## Evidence

**New regression test cases (extending `src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts`):**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts
✓ keeps the overflow daily-cron catch-up deferral after start()'s maintenance pass     (#93810 invariant)
✓ still repairs a stale future cron slot on start() when no jobs were deferred         (#93810 invariant)
✓ keeps the overflow daily-cron deferral after read RPCs (cron list/status) run        (#93935 new)
✓ clears the deferral when the deferred job is removed so the id cannot leak           (#93935 new)
4 passed
```

The new "read RPCs" test is the exact repro from the issue: it builds the same 5 hourly + 1 daily setup, calls `start()`, then calls `status()` and `list()`, and asserts the daily job's `nextRunAtMs` is still `startNow + 5000` rather than the natural tomorrow 09:00 slot. Without the fix this case fails on the assertion `expect(afterRead?.state.nextRunAtMs).toBe(deferredSlot)` because `ensureLoadedForRead` runs maintenance recompute with no skip set.

**Broader cron suites pass unchanged:**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service/ops.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service/timer.test.ts
4 files passed (34 tests)
```

**Typecheck:**

```
$ pnpm tsgo:core         # clean
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test    # clean
```

**Diff size:** 6 files, +118/−2. The fix is entirely additive on the existing `skipFutureRepairJobIds` mechanism — no behavior change for callers that already pass an explicit skip set.

target.title: fix(sessions): defer maintenance preserve scans

## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?
- Session store saves call maintenance preserve providers even when entry maintenance does not need provider state, triggering expensive subagent registry reads on ordinary writes.

Why does this matter now?
- Redacted profiler evidence showed this path on gateway hot workloads.

What is the intended outcome?
